steps.narrationText: Now the fork. One command will clone the entire repo, the app, the database, the config files, regardless of size. One gigabyte, one hundred gigabytes, one terabyte: the size simply does not matter.
Both repos exist side by side now. Same machine, same disk, two completely independent environments.
Let us prove the isolation. In the original, our marker file is right where we left it. The fork existence did not change anything in the parent.
Now let us delete that file, but only inside the fork. We are making a destructive change here.
Back to the original. The marker file is still here, untouched. Same image, separate Docker daemons, separate filesystems.
When you are done, just delete the fork. The original stays exactly as it was. Fork, experiment, break things, delete. No risk.

narrationAudio.narrationText: Welcome to the killer feature! Clone an entire production repo regardless of size, and break nothing.
One parent repo fans out into as many independent clones as you want. Each fork is its own isolated environment running side by side with the original.
Think of sharing a folder link. The link is the same lightweight thing whether the folder is small or huge, and forking works on the same principle.
One gigabyte, one hundred gigabytes, one terabyte: the fork behaves the same. Repo size does not factor into the equation.
The parent repo is the sun and your fork is a mirror catching its light. The shared base stays untouched while anything you paint on the mirror belongs only to you.
A fork is a frozen photograph of the parent at one instant. The parent keeps flowing like a river, but your snapshot stays exactly where it was.
Five forks of a hundred gigabyte repo still add up to about a hundred gigabytes on disk. You only pay for the bytes you actually change inside each fork.
You just saw the commands. Next we make it visual: a real database app, forked live, edited side by side — and the original never flinches.
